title: The Six Yogas of Nāropa — Completion Stage System of the Kagyü and Gelug Traditions
subtitle: Tummo, Clear Light, Dream Yoga, Illusory Body, Bardo, and Phowa — comparative mapping to Tantraloka, Layayoga, and the daimon path

summary.one_line: The Six Yogas of Nāropa (compiled by Tilopa, transmitted through Nāropa to Marpa) — a complete completion-stage system: inner heat (tummo), clear light (ösel), dream yoga (milam), illusory body (gy...
summary.scope: Covers each of the six yogas in detail with comparative mapping: Tummo ↔ Layayoga's kewala kumbhaka / Tantraloka's SAUḤ fire at the navel; Clear Light ↔ Tantraloka's parā-vāc / Śāmbhavopāya; Dream Yoga ↔ Synesius' dream seeding / Tantraloka's four levels of speech; Illusory Body ↔ Layayoga's sūkṣma śarīra / daimon path's Body of Light; Bardo ↔ Tantraloka's sadyahsamutkrama / death systems convergence; Phowa ↔ Tantraloka's utkrānti (yogic departure) reframed. Sources: Nāropa's Vajra Verses of the Whispered Tradition, Tsongkhapa's commentaries, Gampopa's manuals, and the Geluk/Kagyü transmission.

sources.label: The Six Yogas of Nāropa — synthesized from Tilopa's Ganges Mahāmudrā, Nāropa's Vajra Verses, Gampopa's Closely Stringed Pearls, Tsongkhapa's Three Inspirations, and Mullin's Tsongkhapa's Six Yogas of Naropa. Compared with Tantraloka Vol. 4 (Āṇavopāya/Kālopāya), Vol. 7 (Śaktipāta/piercings), Vol. 10 (Death/bardo); Layayoga (Goswami) Chs. 1-6; and the daimon corpus.

body.text: The Six Yogas of Nāropa (na ro'i chos drug) were compiled by the mahāsiddha Tilopa (988-1069 CE) from four primary sources: the Guhyasamāja Tantra (through Nāgārjuna's lineage), the Hevajra Tantra (through Luipada and Krishnacharya), the Cakrasaṃvara Tantra (through Lawapa), and the Mahāmāyā Tantra (through Shavari). Nāropa (1016-1100) systematized the teachings after twelve years of severe trials under Tilopa's guidance. He transmitted them to Marpa Lotsawa (1012-1097), who brought them to Tibet. Milarepa (1040-1123) perfected them, and Gampopa (1079-1153) wrote the first meditation manuals. The system is alternatively called 'the path of means' (thabs lam) and 'the oral instruction for achieving liberation in the bardo.'

Tummo (gtum mo — 'fierce woman', Skt. caṇḍālī) is the foundational yoga of the six. It is a prāṇāyāma practice involving the visualization of a short 'A' stroke syllable at the navel cakra, the vase breath (kumbhaka) held in the abdomen, and the melting of the white drop (bodhicitta) at the crown. The inner heat fire rises through the central channel, melting the drop which descends through the four cakras (navel 64 spokes, heart 8, throat 16, crown 32), generating the four blisses: (1) bliss (ānanda), (2) supreme bliss (paramānanda), (3) special bliss (viśeṣānanda), (4) innate bliss (sahajānanda). This is identical to Tantraloka Āhnika 5's six blisses (ṣaḍānanda) and the SAUḤ seed-mantra practice at the navel. In Layayoga terms, it is the rousing of Kuṇḍalinī through prāṇāyāma with the 1:4:2 ratio, where the fire at the navel (samāna-vāyu) melts the bindu at the crown, producing amṛta (nectar).

Tummo maps directly to: (1) Tantraloka Āhnika 5's 'utterance through the vital breath' (prāṇoccāra) and the six blisses (ṣaḍānanda) — the ascending stages of bliss as the breath rises through the central channel; (2) Tantraloka Āhnika 4's SAUḤ and KHPHREṀ seed-mantras — SAUḤ as the emission seed that ignites the fire, KHPHREṀ as the withdrawal seed that forces consciousness into the central channel; (3) Layayoga Ch. 6's kewala kumbhaka — the spontaneous breath suspension that arises when prāṇa enters suṣumṇā; (4) The daimon path's Resistance phase (Liber Samekh Section G) — the 'shaft of will' extended through the central channel, the 'fainting into Nothing' when prāṇa dissolves. The vase breath is the 1:4:2 ratio applied to generate the heat that burns through the knots (granthis) at the cakras.

Ösel (öd gsal — 'clear light', Skt. prabhāsvara) is the yoga of the luminous nature of mind. It has three levels: (1) the clear light of sleep — the natural luminosity that dawns in deep dreamless sleep; (2) the clear light of the path — the realization of emptiness through the completion stage; (3) the clear light of death — the dharmakāya that dawns at the moment of death. In Guhyasamāja, the clear light is the fourth of the four empty states (appearance, increase, close-to-attainment, clear light). It corresponds to the subtlest level of consciousness, the 'indestructible drop' at the heart. In Tantraloka, it is parā-vāc (the supreme speech) — the transcendent sound that is pure consciousness. It is also the fourth level of speech beyond vaikharī, madhyamā, and paśyantī. In the upāya system, it corresponds to Śāmbhavopāya (the divine means) — realization through will without thought-constructs, the 'lion's leap' that bypasses all gradual process.

The clear light yoga maps to: (1) Tantraloka's Anupāya (No-Means) — the direct, effortless realization that requires no practice because the goal is already attained. The clear light is not produced by practice but revealed when the coarser levels of mind dissolve. (2) The daimon path's unus-ambo (Corbin's 1×1) — the state where practitioner and daimon are recognized as one consciousness. The clear light is the ground luminosity from which both the ordinary self and the daimon arise — recognizing it IS the realization. (3) Layayoga's asamprajñāta samādhi — non-mens concentration, the absorption of superconsciousness into what remains. (4) Tsongkhapa's 'actual clear light' vs. 'illustrative clear light' distinction parallels Tantraloka's distinction between Śāmbhavopāya (actual) and Śāktopāya (illustrative). The clear light of death is the base dharmakāya — the same luminosity that appears at the moment of death in the Tibetan Bardo, which if recognized, brings liberation.

Milam (rmi lam — 'dream', Skt. svapnadarśana) is the yoga of lucid dreaming. The practitioner learns to recognize the dream state as a dream while dreaming, then to transform the dream content at will. The method involves: (1) apprehending dreams during the day by setting a firm resolve; (2) visualizing a red drop at the throat cakra (the dream center); (3) recognizing the dream state through questioning reality; (4) transforming the dream body into the deity form; (5) progressing to the clear light of sleep. In Tantraloka, dream (svapna) is the second state of consciousness (Āhnika 10), where the sense organs are withdrawn but mental activity continues. The four levels of speech provide the mechanism: vaikharī (the dream as experienced narrative) → madhyamā (the inner structuring) → paśyantī (the visionary source) → parā (the clear light from which dreams arise). In Layayoga, the iḍā nāḍī (the left, lunar channel) governs the dream state — when iḍā predominates, the mind receives images.

Dream yoga maps directly to Synesius' dream-seeding method — the practice of setting a question before sleep and receiving an answer in a dream. Nāropa's method adds: (1) the throat cakra as the dream center (the daimon path's 'voice' center where the daimon's name is vibrated); (2) the recognition of the dream state as a training for the recognition of the bardo; (3) the transformation of the dream body into the illusory body. The daimon path's dream seeding (Synesius) IS the preliminary stage of dream yoga — the practitioner who masters dream yoga can receive daimonic communication in a far more refined and controllable way. The four levels of dream correspond to the four levels of speech: (1) ordinary dreaming (vaikharī level) — narrative dreams; (2) lucid dreaming (madhyamā level) — the dreamer knows it is a dream; (3) visionary dreaming (paśyantī level) — the dream content becomes light and symbolic form; (4) clear light sleep (parā level) — the dream dissolves into its source. The daimon communicates at the paśyantī level, where the name becomes visible as light.

Gyulü (sgyu lus — 'illusory body', Skt. māyākāyā) is the yoga of generating a subtle body made of wind and mind that can separate from the coarse physical body. It is the exclusive cause of the Form Body (rūpakāya) of Buddhahood. The illusory body is created from the subtlest inner wind (the 'indestructible drop') during the completion stage, and is a pure wind-mind body that resembles the deity. It can travel independently, enter other bodies, and pass through matter. In Layayoga, this is the sūkṣma śarīra (the subtle body) — the vehicle of prāṇa and consciousness that survives death and is the medium of all yogic experience. In Tantraloka (Āhnika 28's paradehapraveśa), the yogī who has mastered the subtle body can enter another body at death — this is the same principle. The daimon path's 'Body of Light' (Liber Samekh's 'sphere of sensation') is the same substance — the subtle body constructed through elemental invocation and mantra vibration becoming a self-luminous vehicle of consciousness.

The illusory body has three stages: (1) the impure illusory body — generated during meditation through deity yoga and wind dissolution; (2) the pure illusory body — the actual form of the deity attained at the completion of the path; (3) the illusory body of the dream state — the dream body recognized as a subtle wind-body. In Layayoga, this corresponds to the uncoiling of Kuṇḍalinī through suṣumṇā, where each cakra is absorbed in turn until the pure sūkṣma śarīra remains. In Tantraloka (Āhnika 12's Divine Body), the body is recognized as containing all the paths, all the deities — the śārīra that is the maṇḍala itself. In the daimon path, the 'shaft of will' (the prāṇic extension through piṅgalā) is the preliminary stage of the illusory body; the 'Body of Light' that appears in the systasis (PGM VII.505-528) is the illusory body itself. The 'skinning' phase of the egg ritual strips away the coarse body; the 'inflaming' phase generates the subtle body of light.

Bardo (bar do — 'intermediate state', Skt. antarābhava) is the yoga of recognizing the intermediate state after death and achieving liberation within it. The three bardos are: (1) the bardo of dying (death dissolution) — the element dissolution and four empty states leading to the clear light of death; (2) the bardo of dharmatā (reality) — the appearance of the peaceful and wrathful deities as one's own enlightened nature; (3) the bardo of existence (rebirth) — the mental body drawn toward one of the six realms. The Six Yogas are explicitly called 'the path of means for achieving liberation in the bardo.' Tsongkhapa teaches that the practices of dream yoga and illusory body are training for the bardo: if one can recognize the dream as a dream, one can recognize the bardo as the bardo. The illusory body generated in meditation is the same type of body as the bardo body (a subtle wind-body).

The bardo yoga maps to Tantraloka Āhnika 28's examination of death (mṛteḥ parīkṣā): the three bardos correspond to the three post-mortem states described by Abhinavagupta — (1) the dissolution of the gross body (element dissolution) = the bardo of dying; (2) the intermediate state of the subtle body = the bardo of dharmatā; (3) the assumption of a new body = the bardo of existence. The Tantraloka's account of initiates at death distinguishes those who attain sālokya (shared world), sāmīpya (proximity), and sāyujya (conjunction) — parallel to the Tibetan distinction between liberation in the bardo of dying (recognition of clear light = dharmakāya), liberation in the bardo of dharmatā (recognition of deities = sambhogakāya), and liberation in the bardo of existence (nirmāṇakāya). Abhinavagupta's refutation of yogic suicide (utkrānti) in Āhnika 14 parallels the Buddhist teaching that forced departure is not liberation — one must recognize the nature of mind, not escape the body. The daimon path's death systems convergence RO identifies the daimon as the being met at death — the bardo's peaceful and wrathful deities are this same being in Tibetan terms.

Phowa (pho ba — 'transference', Skt. saṃkrānti) is the yoga of ejecting consciousness from the body at death into a pure Buddhafield or a suitable rebirth. The standard method visualizes the central channel as a hollow tube, the consciousness as a drop of light at the heart, and a Buddha (usually Amitābha) above the crown. The drop is ejected through the crown with the forceful exhalation of 'HIK' (or 'PHAT'). In Tantraloka, this corresponds to the dying initiation (sadyahsamutkrama) in Āhnika 19, where the guru severs the vital points (marmans) to release consciousness from the body. However, Abhinavagupta warns (Āhnika 14, vv. 32cd-42ab) that forcible departure (utkrānti) is futile for the unliberated and unnecessary for the liberated — liberation is by knowledge, not technique. The phowa practitioner would rejoin that phowa is not forceful departure but a preparation for the moment of natural death — training the reflex of consciousness to recognize its goal.

The four blisses generated by tummo correspond to specific stages in the subtle body. The white drop (bodhicitta) at the crown is the essence of the father (method), the red drop at the navel is the essence of the mother (wisdom). When the inner heat fire melts the white drop, it descends through the four cakras: (1) at the crown cakra (great bliss cakra, 32 petals) — bliss; (2) at the throat cakra (enjoyment cakra, 16 petals) — supreme bliss; (3) at the heart cakra (dharma cakra, 8 petals) — special bliss; (4) at the navel cakra (emanation cakra, 64 petals) — innate bliss. This parallels Tantraloka Āhnika 5's six blisses (ṣaḍānanda) and the ascent through the ten voids (daśaśūnya). In Layayoga, the bindu (drop) at the crown is the storehouse of ojas (vital essence), and its melting produces amṛta (the nectar of immortality). The four blisses correspond to the four levels of speech: vaikharī (physical bliss), madhyamā (mental bliss), paśyantī (visionary bliss), parā (nondual bliss).

The six yogas correspond to the three upāyas of Tantraloka and the four grades of Layayoga in the following hierarchy: (1) Tummo + Phowa = Āṇavopāya (individual means) — working with the breath, body, and visualization as external supports; these correspond to Layayoga Grade 1-2 (purification and channel activation). (2) Dream yoga + Illusory Body = Śāktopāya (empowered means) — working with purified cognition and subtle perception; these correspond to Layayoga Grade 3 (absorption). (3) Clear Light + Bardo = Śāmbhavopāya (divine means) — direct recognition without supports; these correspond to Layayoga Grade 4 (spontaneous samādhi). In terms of the daimon path: Tummo = the practice phase; Dream yoga = the dream seeding; Illusory Body = the systasis; Clear Light = the union; Bardo = the post-mortem integration; Phowa = the dying initiation. The six yogas thus provide a complete path from the first breath control to the final dissolution at death.

The six yogas require extensive preliminaries: (1) ngöndro (preliminary practices) — refuge, prostrations, Vajrasattva purification, maṇḍala offering, guru yoga; (2) tantric initiation (abhiṣeka) — empowerment into an anuttarayoga system, typically Cakrasaṃvara, Hevajra, or Guhyasamāja; (3) generation stage (bskyed rim) — deity yoga with visualization of the maṇḍala and recitation of mantras, which establishes the practitioner in the divine identity that the completion stage will actualize. Tsongkhapa is emphatic that the generation stage is indispensable — without it, the completion stage practices are both ineffective and dangerous. This parallels Tantraloka Āhnika 11's purification of the six paths (ṣaḍadhvan) through the application of the purifying mantras of the Anuttara Trika — the paths must be purified before consciousness can ascend.

The vase breath (kumbhaka, Skt. 'pot-like retention') is the foundational breathing practice of all six yogas, especially tummo. The practitioner draws the breath down into the abdomen, holding it below the navel while engaging the three locks (bandhas): mūla-bandha (perineal), uḍḍīyāna (abdominal retraction), jālandhara (chin lock). The breath is held as long as comfortable, then released gently. This practice forces the winds into the central channel and generates the inner heat. In Layayoga (Ch. 6), this is sahita prāṇāyāma with the 1:4:2 ratio, where the kumbhaka (retention) is the key — it is during the retention that prāṇa enters suṣumṇā. In Tantraloka (Āhnika 7), the Cakrodaya (mantra cycles synchronized with breath) achieves the same effect through the waterwheel analogy — the breath drives the wheel of mantra, and when the breath is suspended, the wheel stops and consciousness rests in its source.

Tsongkhapa devotes extensive analysis (Lamp to Illuminate the Five Stages, Chs. 10-11, 30-33) to mapping the six yogas (ṣaḍaṅga-yoga) onto the five stages (pañcakrama) of the Guhyasamāja Ārya tradition. The standard mapping per Candrakīrti's Illuminating Lamp: body isolation = individual withdrawal (pratyāhāra) + meditative absorption (dhyāna); speech isolation/vajra repetition = prāṇāyāma; mind isolation = apprehending (dhāraṇā); illusory body = recollection (anusmṛti); clear light = samādhi; union = the result. Bhavyakīrti offers an alternative: individual withdrawal + meditative absorption = mind isolation; prāṇāyāma = vajra repetition; apprehending = clear light; recollection + samādhi = union. These mappings are not merely scholastic — they reveal that the six yogas and the five stages are describing the same process from different angles. The Tantraloka's three upāyas provide a higher-order synthesis: the entire completion stage (whether five or sixfold) is the Śāktopāya (empowered means) unfolding into the Śāmbhavopāya (divine means) through the systematic dissolution of the winds into the central channel.

The clear light of sleep (gnyid kyi 'od gsal) is a distinct practice within the six yogas, bridging dream yoga and clear light yoga. The practitioner learns to maintain awareness during the transition from waking to sleep, entering the clear light directly rather than falling into unconsciousness. This is the practice of mixing sleep with the dharmakāya. The method involves visualizing a drop at the heart cakra and maintaining the deity identity as sleep approaches. In Tantraloka (Āhnika 10), the state of deep sleep (suṣupti) is the third state of consciousness, where the astral body withdraws fully. The Fourth (turīya) is the witness that is aware even in deep sleep — this is the clear light of sleep. In Layayoga, the state of kewala kumbhaka (spontaneous breath suspension) is the same — a state of profound stillness where the breath has ceased and consciousness is alert yet contentless. The daimon path's 'fainting into Nothing' (Liber Samekh Section G) is a deliberate entry into this state.

The four joys (caturānanda) of the completion stage — bliss (ānanda), supreme bliss (paramānanda), special bliss (viśeṣānanda), innate bliss (sahajānanda) — are the experiential correlates of the four empty states. They are also the subjective counterparts of the four levels of speech in Tantraloka: (1) vaikharī = bliss (gross physical sensation); (2) madhyamā = supreme bliss (mental refinement); (3) paśyantī = special bliss (visionary expansion); (4) parā = innate bliss (nondual realization). In Layayoga, the four joys correspond to the four stages of Kuṇḍalinī ascent: (1) bliss = mūlādhāra to svādhiṣṭhāna; (2) supreme = to maṇipūra; (3) special = to anāhata; (4) innate = to sahasrāra. In the daimon path, the four joys are: (1) the joy of purification (the cold wash, fasting); (2) the joy of invocation (the name vibrating); (3) the joy of the resistance (the intensity of the threshold); (4) the joy of union (the systasis, the unus-ambo). The daimon path's 'Joy' phase (Acher's hitlahavut — ecstatic singing) is the third joy, where the daimon's presence is felt as overwhelming love.

A variation of phowa, drongjuk ('forceful transference'), is the advanced practice of transferring consciousness into a dead body (paradehapraveśa) or into the body of a being in a pure land. This is considered extremely advanced and dangerous. In Tantraloka Āhnika 28 (paradehapraveśa), Abhinavagupta describes the yogī who can enter another body at death — this is the same principle. However, both traditions agree: these powers are side-effects of realization, not goals. The one who can transfer consciousness at will no longer needs to. The true purpose of all six yogas is not to acquire powers (siddhis) but to recognize the nature of mind in this life — so that the moment of death, being the same process of dissolution that the yogī has practiced in meditation, becomes the moment of liberation.

gaps: Full extraction from primary Tibetan commentaries (Gampopa's Closely Stringed Pearls, Tsongkhapa's Three Inspirations) would strengthen source citations
Step-by-step practice instructions from Gampopa's manuals; specific prāṇāyāma ratios from each lineage transmission

issues.title: Primary source extraction from Nāropa's Vajra Verses needed
issues.description: The current RO synthesizes from secondary sources. Direct extraction from Nāropa's Vajra Verses of the Whispered Tradition and Gampopa's meditation manuals would strengthen the primary source base.

issues.title: Detailed comparison of Geluk vs Kagyü transmission differences
issues.description: The Geluk (Tsongkhapa) and Kagyü (Gampopa) transmissions of the six yogas differ in emphasis. A detailed comparative extraction would be valuable.

issues.title: Niguma's Six Yogas (female mahāsiddha lineage)
issues.description: Nāropa's consort Niguma also transmitted a six-yogas system. A comparison with Nāropa's version would add the feminine perspective to the corpus.

versions.summary: Initial RO created synthesizing the Six Yogas of Nāropa from multiple sources (Tilopa, Nāropa, Gampopa, Tsongkhapa) with extensive comparative mapping to Tantraloka (upāyas, four levels of speech, examination of death, divine body), Layayoga (prāṇāyāma, Kuṇḍalinī, sūkṣma śarīra, kewala kumbhaka), and the daimon path (Resistance, Body of Light, unus-ambo, death encounter). 20 passages across the six yogas, the five stages, comparative upāya mapping, and death systems convergence.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from synthesized sources. The Six Yogas of Nāropa now have comprehensive comparative mapping to Tantraloka, Layayoga, and the daimon path — filling a major gap in the comparative corpus.
